questions.q: Which Indian mathematician is famously associated with the number 1729?
questions.explanation: Srinivasa Ramanujan instantly recognized 1729's special property, giving rise to the famous anecdote.

questions.q: What is special about the number 1729?
options: It is the largest known Fibonacci number
It is the smallest number expressible as a sum of two cubes in two different ways
questions.explanation: 1729 is the smallest positive integer that can be written as a sum of two positive cubes in two distinct ways.

questions.q: The number 1729 is commonly known by what nickname?
questions.explanation: It is called the Hardy-Ramanujan number after the famous conversation between the two mathematicians.

questions.q: Which British mathematician was visiting Ramanujan when the 1729 anecdote took place?
questions.explanation: G. H. Hardy recounted arriving in a taxicab numbered 1729 and finding the number dull, prompting Ramanujan's reply.

questions.q: Because of the taxicab in the famous story, numbers like 1729 are also called what?
questions.explanation: Numbers expressible as a sum of two cubes in multiple ways are known as taxicab numbers, after Hardy's taxi.

questions.q: Ramanujan, associated with 1729, was a mathematician from which country?
questions.explanation: Srinivasa Ramanujan was an Indian mathematician, born in Erode in Tamil Nadu.

questions.q: In the famous story, why did Hardy say the number 1729 seemed uninteresting?
options: It seemed a rather dull number with no obvious special property
questions.explanation: Hardy remarked that 1729 seemed a dull number, hoping it was not an unfavourable omen.

questions.q: The operation central to the property of 1729 involves adding two of what?
questions.explanation: 1729 is famous as a sum of two cubes expressible in two different ways.

questions.q: At which English university did the Hardy-Ramanujan collaboration mainly take place?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan worked with Hardy at the University of Cambridge, primarily at Trinity College.

questions.q: The number 1729 is most closely connected to which branch of mathematics?
questions.explanation: The property of being a sum of two cubes in multiple ways belongs to number theory.

questions.q: Which pair of cubes sums to 1729 using the smaller cube roots?
questions.explanation: 1729 = 1³ + 12³ = 1 + 1728, one of its two distinct cube representations.

questions.q: Which is the second pair of cubes that also sums to 1729?
questions.explanation: 1729 = 9³ + 10³ = 729 + 1000, the second of its two distinct representations.

questions.q: In what year did the famous 1729 hospital conversation between Hardy and Ramanujan reportedly occur?
questions.explanation: Hardy's account places the visit around 1918-1919, while Ramanujan was ill in a nursing home near London.

questions.q: The general numbers expressible as a sum of two positive cubes in n different ways are denoted Ta(n). What is Ta(2)?
questions.explanation: Ta(2) = 1729 is the smallest number expressible as a sum of two positive cubes in two ways.

questions.q: What was the mathematical specialty for which Ramanujan is most renowned?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan made profound contributions to number theory, infinite series, continued fractions and partitions.

questions.q: Which honour did Ramanujan receive in 1918, making him one of the youngest to be so elected at the time?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1918 for his work in elliptic functions and number theory.

questions.q: In which Indian city was Ramanujan working as a clerk before Hardy invited him to Cambridge?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan worked at the Madras Port Trust before his correspondence with Hardy led to Cambridge.

questions.q: 1729 is also a Harshad number, meaning it is divisible by what?
questions.explanation: A Harshad number is divisible by the sum of its digits; 1+7+2+9 = 19, and 1729 = 19 × 91.

questions.q: The next taxicab number after 1729, expressible as a sum of two positive cubes in two ways, is which of these?
questions.explanation: 4104 = 2³ + 16³ = 9³ + 15³ is the second-smallest such number after 1729.

questions.q: Ramanujan's date of birth, 22 December, is now commemorated in India as which day?
questions.explanation: India observes National Mathematics Day on 22 December to mark Ramanujan's birth anniversary.

questions.q: What is the prime factorization of 1729?
questions.explanation: 1729 = 7 × 13 × 19, the product of three distinct primes.

questions.q: Which 2015 biographical film about Ramanujan helped popularize the 1729 story to wider audiences?
questions.explanation: The Man Who Knew Infinity, based on Robert Kanigel's biography, dramatizes Ramanujan's life and the 1729 anecdote.

questions.q: Long before 1729 became famous, the French mathematician who noted it as the smallest sum of two cubes in two ways (in 1657) was?
questions.explanation: Bernard Frénicle de Bessy recorded this property of 1729 in 1657, long before the Hardy-Ramanujan anecdote.

questions.q: The sum of the digits of 1729 is 19, and 1729 = 19 × 91. What is special about the second factor, 91?
options: It is the digits of the digit-sum (19) written in reverse
questions.explanation: 1+7+2+9 = 19, and 1729 = 19 × 91; the factor 91 is simply the digits of 19 reversed.

questions.q: 1729 is a Carmichael number, a composite number that fools Fermat's primality test for every base coprime to it. For this reason a Carmichael number is also called an absolute what?
questions.explanation: A Carmichael number such as 1729 is an absolute Fermat pseudoprime, passing Fermat's primality test for every base coprime to it.

questions.q: The taxicab number Ta(3) = 87539319 is the smallest expressible as a sum of two positive cubes in how many distinct ways?
questions.explanation: By definition Ta(3) is the smallest number expressible as a sum of two positive cubes in three different ways.

questions.q: Ramanujan reportedly hinted that 1729 relates to a near-counterexample of Fermat's Last Theorem because 1³ + 12³ is very close to which cube?
questions.explanation: 12³ = 1728, just one less than 1729, illustrating how close 1³ + 12³ comes to another cube.

questions.q: Generalizing the taxicab idea, numbers that are a sum of two cubes in n ways with the cubes allowed to be negative are called what?
questions.explanation: Cabtaxi numbers allow positive and negative cubes, and the smallest non-trivial cabtaxi number is 91.

questions.q: Which mathematician rediscovered Ramanujan's 'lost notebook' in 1976, reviving interest in his unpublished work?
questions.explanation: George Andrews found Ramanujan's lost notebook in the Wren Library at Trinity College, Cambridge, in 1976.

questions.q: At what young age did Ramanujan, the mathematician behind the 1729 story, die in 1920?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan died in 1920 at the age of 32, after returning to India from England in poor health.
